translation: The Inevitable Reality
commentary:
ٱلْحَآقَّةُ

The surah opens with a single, powerful word: Al-Haqqah. This word comes from the root ح-ق-ق, which means truth, but carries a deeper significance. Haqq refers to that which is rightfully deserved, that which must materialize. When the Quran uses the verb form حَقَّتْ, it describes how punishment materialized against a people - not merely that it occurred, but that it rightfully came upon them as something bound to happen.

The grammatical form al-Haqqah uses the muballagha pattern, which intensifies the meaning to convey "the ultimate reality" or "that which is absolutely bound to occur." This is reality itself in its most profound sense - the inevitable consequences that must unfold.

Notice how this ayah presents only a subject (mubtada) without its predicate (khabar). In Arabic rhetoric, when you begin a sentence but leave it incomplete, you immediately capture your audience's attention. They're left anticipating: where is the rest of this sentence? What about this reality? This technique creates suspense and urgency.

The use of the ism (noun form) rather than the verb form is also significant. While both can express similar meanings, the ism carries immediacy - like the difference between saying "I'm coming" versus "I'm right here, coming now." Al-Haqqah isn't some distant future event; it's presented as imminent, right around the corner.

translation: What is the Inevitable Reality
commentary:
مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ

The incompleteness of the first ayah is intensified here. "What is Al-Haqqah?" Allah ﷻ restarts the sentence entirely, posing it now as a question. The listener's curiosity peaks further - not only was the first statement incomplete, but now a question is raised about this mysterious reality.

This rhetorical device serves to emphasize how beyond human comprehension this reality truly is. The question itself suggests that simply mentioning Al-Haqqah isn't sufficient; we need to understand what it actually means.

translation: And what can make you know what is the Inevitable Reality
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحَآقَّةُ

"And what could possibly give you any clue what Al-Haqqah is?" The verb أَدْرَىٰكَ means to give an indication or clue, to help someone comprehend even slightly. This question appears throughout the Quran when Allah ﷻ wants to emphasize that something is beyond our ability to fully grasp.

This pattern - where Allah ﷻ asks a question that only He can answer - signals that divine explanation is about to follow. We're being told: there's no way you could understand this on your own, so let Me show you.

What is this ultimate reality? It encompasses both dimensions of divine warning that every prophet brought to his people. First, there are consequences in this world - nations that reject their messengers face destruction here and now. Second, there's the Day of Judgment - the ultimate accountability for all of humanity. Understanding these two types of warnings is central to grasping the message of all prophets, including our beloved Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

The Prophet ﷺ came with both warnings, just as all prophets did before him. The Makkans would face consequences in this life, and they - along with all humanity - would face the final reckoning in the Hereafter. This dual nature of warning is essential to understand, because the ayat about fighting disbelievers, when properly contextualized, were part of Allah's final punishment against the Makkans who rejected the Messenger ﷺ in the flesh - just as previous nations faced their specific punishments. These verses aren't meant to be understood as the general foreign policy of Islam, but rather as fulfillment of Allah's consistent promise: when a nation rejects its messenger, consequences follow in this world.

translation: Thamud and 'Aad denied the Striking Calamity
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ وَعَادٌۢ بِٱلْقَارِعَةِ

Allah ﷻ begins to answer the question by providing historical examples. "Thamud and 'Ad denied Al-Qari'ah." The word الْقَارِعَةُ comes from a root meaning to strike, to bang, to knock loudly - like someone pounding on a door or ramming something into it with tremendous force. It's the kind of sound that jolts you awake, that doesn't let you rest.

While Al-Haqqah is the broader term for ultimate reality encompassing all consequences, Al-Qari'ah is more specific - it refers to that striking, jolting destruction that came upon these nations. Imagine the terror: you're in your home, feeling safe, when suddenly rocks begin raining from the sky. The constant banging, the relentless pounding against your walls and ceiling. Picture a thunderstorm or tornado where debris hurls through the air, slamming into windows and walls. That incessant, terrifying knocking growing louder and closer - this is the reality of Al-Qari'ah.

These nations were warned by their prophets about this striking punishment, just as we're being warned about Al-Haqqah. But they كَذَّبَتْ - they didn't just disbelieve, they actively denied and called it a lie.

translation: So as for Thamud, they were destroyed by the overpowering [blast]
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِٱلطَّاغِيَةِ

"As for Thamud, they were destroyed by الطَّاغِيَةِ." The passive verb form أُهْلِكُوا۟ - "they were destroyed" - leaves the agent unstated, though we know it's Allah ﷻ Who destroyed them. This grammatical choice emphasizes the state of their destruction rather than the Actor.

The word الطَّاغِيَةِ carries profound meaning. The preposition بِ here can be understood in two ways. First, as indicating cause: they were destroyed because of الطَّاغِيَةِ - because of their excessive rebellion, their transgression beyond all bounds. Second, as indicating means: they were destroyed by means of a punishment that itself was rebellious.

This second meaning reveals something remarkable about Allah's justice. Consider how Allah ﷻ has subdued the elements for us - the earth beneath our feet is described in the Quran like a domesticated animal we ride upon. The gentle breeze we enjoy exists because the winds are held back, controlled. The calm ocean waves show waters that have been subdued. These are all "tamed" for our benefit.

But what happens when these elements are released from their restraints? The winds become tornadoes, the oceans turn into tsunamis and floods, the earth produces earthquakes that swallow entire civilizations. The word طَغَىٰ means to rebel, to transgress boundaries - and it applies both to people and to these natural forces. When domesticated animals rebel, they break free of control. When the ocean rebels (and Arabic actually calls floods الطغيان - the rebellion of water), it crosses its shores, transgressing its natural boundaries.

The beauty of this ayah is its dual meaning: the nation of Thamud was destroyed because of their rebellion, and when they rebelled against Allah ﷻ, He allowed the very elements of nature to rebel against them. You want to cross the line? Fine - I'll let the earth, the water, and the sky cross their lines too.

translation: And as for 'Aad, they were destroyed by a screaming, violent wind
commentary:
وَأَمَّا عَادٌ فَأُهْلِكُوا۟ بِرِيحٍ صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ

"And as for 'Ad, they were destroyed by a صَرْصَرٍ عَاتِيَةٍ wind" - a wind that was sar-sar and 'atiyah. Each descriptor adds layers to this terrifying image.

صَرْصَرٍ describes bitterly, intensely cold wind. But it's more than just cold - the doubled syllables (sar-sar) indicate repetition, a relentless quality. This wasn't wind that you feel once and then grow accustomed to. Every single gust that hit you would freeze you again, and again, and again. Each blast was like experiencing that shocking cold for the first time, over and over. The structure of the word itself mirrors the experience - repetitive, unrelenting, each strike of cold as sharp as the last.

عَاتِيَةٍ means transgressing, going beyond limits, crossing boundaries. Picture people sheltering in their homes with doors and windows shut, trying to protect themselves from the wind outside. But this wind didn't stop at walls - it went right through them. The usual boundaries meant nothing. The winds penetrated their homes and froze them even in what should have been their place of safety.

The verb سَخَّرَهَا - "He subdued it" - uses the same word Allah ﷻ uses for His control over the clouds, making them move forward or hold back, making them rain here or there. It's the word used when you've finally mastered a wild horse, and it responds to your every command. These winds weren't just blowing randomly - they were under Allah's direct control, targeting the criminals with precision. The winds were weaponized, aimed specifically at those who had transgressed.

translation: Which Allah imposed upon them for seven nights and eight days in succession, so you would see the people therein fallen as if they were hollow trunks of palm trees
commentary:
سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا فَتَرَى ٱلْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ

"He imposed it upon them for seven nights and eight days, حُسُومًا." Think about what we know of natural disasters today. Modern tornadoes tear through towns in minutes - two, maybe three minutes - and leave catastrophic destruction in their wake. Entire neighborhoods reduced to nothing but planks of wood. I've witnessed this devastation firsthand in Arlington, Texas, and in New Orleans after Hurricane Katrina. A massive masjid reduced to rubble, just piles of wooden planks where a building once stood.

If a tornado can accomplish this level of destruction in minutes, imagine seven nights and eight days of continuous, unrelenting wind. We evacuate for storms that last a few hours. We take shelter, we wait it out. But a week? An entire week of non-stop, targeted, freezing winds that penetrate through walls? This is beyond our ability to fully comprehend.

The word حُسُومًا intensifies this image even further. It comes from a root meaning to erase completely, to remove all traces. This wasn't just destruction - this was total obliteration. By the time these winds finished, there was no sign left that a civilization had ever existed there. Complete erasure.

فَتَرَى ٱلْقَوْمَ فِيهَا صَرْعَىٰ - "Then you would see the people in it, صَرْعَىٰ." This word describes bodies lying motionless, but also carries the meaning of being struck down, lying twisted and mangled. Not peaceful rest, but bodies toppled over, contorted, scattered about in unnatural positions.

كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ خَاوِيَةٍ - "As if they were hollowed-out trunks of palm trees." The word أَعْجَازُ refers to the lower part, the base or trunk, while خَاوِيَةٍ means hollow, empty, devoid of life. Picture dead palm trees - once strong and tall, now just empty bark and brittle branches, fallen and scattered. This is how the mighty people of 'Ad ended up: once a powerful nation, now reduced to lifeless forms, hollow shells scattered across the landscape like the dried, emptied trunks of dead trees.

This vivid imagery serves as the answer to the question "What is Al-Haqqah?" It's this - the inevitable reality where arrogance and denial lead to complete destruction, where rebellion meets its match in the rebellion of nature itself, where the powerful are reduced to nothing. This is what these nations called a lie, and this is what came upon them as an undeniable, inescapable truth.

translation: Then do you see of them any remains
commentary:
فَهَلْ تَرَىٰ لَهُم مِّنۢ بَاقِيَةٍ

The ayah paints a haunting picture of complete devastation. The people destroyed by Allah's punishment appear like scattered pieces of wood lying on the ground. Only upon closer inspection would someone realize these are actually human beings – this was their final fate.

The question "Did you see anybody left after Allah ﷻ was done?" employs a rhetorical structure that goes beyond simple inquiry. The phrase بَاقِيَةٍ means "remaining" or "left over." The question isn't merely asking if anyone survived – it's more profound than that. The earth itself, as a living entity that knows its own secrets, couldn't find a single survivor. This rhetorical device emphasizes the totality of the destruction: not just that no one survived, but that even the land itself searched and found no one.

translation: And there came Pharaoh and those before him and the overturned cities with sin
commentary:
وَجَآءَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَن قَبْلَهُۥ وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَـٰتُ بِٱلْخَاطِئَةِ

The ayah shifts to mention specific examples of destroyed nations. Pharaoh and those who came before him are mentioned, along with المؤتفكات – the overturned cities. This term المؤتفكات comes from the root أفك, which means to fabricate lies or to turn people's opinions in the wrong direction for corrupt purposes. The word specifically refers to nations that were completely turned over, most notably the people of Prophet Lut (AS).

These nations came with الخاطئة – a huge mistake, a grave error. What was this mistake? The following ayah makes it clear.

translation: And they disobeyed the messenger of their Lord, so He seized them with a seizure exceeding [in severity]
commentary:
فَعَصَوْا۟ رَسُولَ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَهُمْ أَخْذَةً رَّابِيَةً

Their fundamental error was disobedience: they disobeyed the messenger of their Master. The consequence is described with vivid intensity: فَأَخَذَهُمْ – He seized them, grabbed them. But what kind of seizing was this?

The word رَّابِيَةً is particularly striking. It comes from the root meaning growth and increase. Imagine someone grabbing your hand and beginning to squeeze. At first it doesn't hurt much. You might say "it's okay, it doesn't hurt." But they keep squeezing, and it keeps getting worse. Eventually you're pleading "Okay, okay, stop! Please stop!" But the squeeze doesn't stop – it keeps getting tighter and tighter, continuously growing in intensity until it becomes unbearable, and even then it continues.

This is the nature of Allah's punishment described here: a grip that continuously intensifies, that keeps crushing more and more without cessation. For those destroyed by the flood, the water itself crushed them with this ever-increasing pressure.

translation: Indeed, when the water overflowed, We carried your ancestors in the sailing ship
commentary:
إِنَّا لَمَّا طَغَا ٱلْمَآءُ حَمَلْنَـٰكُمْ فِى ٱلْجَارِيَةِ

The ayah addresses a remarkable reality: when the water rebelled – when the great flood occurred – Allah ﷻ says "We boarded you onto the ships." Not "them" but "you." Why this direct address to people living thousands of years after the flood?

Because every human being alive today is a survivor of that flood. The only humanity to continue after the flood of Nuh (AS) were those people on the ark. All human beings being addressed in the Quran are actually descendants and extensions of those survivors. This is why Allah ﷻ can say "We boarded you" – because our very existence depends on that boarding, on that survival. If people hadn't survived the flood on that ship, none of us would be here.

translation: That We might make it for you a reminder and [that] a conscious ear would be conscious of it
commentary:
لِنَجْعَلَهَا لَكُمْ تَذْكِرَةً وَتَعِيَهَآ أُذُنٌ وَٰعِيَةٌ

Allah ﷻ made this event a powerful reminder. The point isn't simply that finding the physical ark would remind us – whether the ark of Nuh (AS) is ever discovered or not is irrelevant. The reminder is the profound reality that you and I are actually survivors of the Great Flood. Our very existence is evidence of that divine intervention and mercy.

This is why the story of the flood appears across human civilizations and religions. Everyone knows about the flood of Nuh (AS) – even Hindu traditions preserve a version calling him "Maha Nu" (the Great Nuh). It's embedded in human memory because it's the story of human survival, of how our population on this earth continued. The Jewish and Christian traditions preserve this account in their scriptures.

Tragically, in modern times, nothing remains sacred. What was once a profound historical and spiritual reality has been reduced to entertainment – comedy movies, cartoons, jokes. The Pink Panther building an ark becomes comedy. Recent films treat it as folklore or spectacle.

But Allah ﷻ asks: what kind of ears will truly hear this story? The phrase وَتَعِيَهَآ أُذُنٌ وَٰعِيَةٌ speaks of ears that are deeply attentive and aware. The word وَٰعِيَةٌ comes from a root meaning to think about something very carefully, to listen with thorough awareness and attention. These are ears that don't hear the story as mere entertainment or ancient folklore, but truly understand and take caution from its profound lessons.

translation: Then when the Horn is blown with one blast
commentary:
فَإِذَا نُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ نَفْخَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ

A dramatic transition occurs here. Until now, the surah has described the first category of punishment – the destruction of nations in this world, punishments that occurred in history (Ad, Thamud, the people of Pharaoh, the people of Lut). This was Phase One: worldly destruction.

Now Phase Two begins: the ultimate accounting. When a single blow is blown into the trumpet, a different kind of destruction unfolds. What we learn from this structure is significant – the ultimate reality (Al-Haqqah) is actually a two-part reality. The destruction in this world serves as a prelude and warning for the far greater destruction and resurrection to come.

translation: And the earth and the mountains are lifted and leveled with one blow
commentary:
وَحُمِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ فَدُكَّتَا دَكَّةً وَٰحِدَةً

Enormous chunks of the earth are lifted into the air. Entire mountains are raised up before the eyes of human beings. Then comes دُكَّتَا – they are smashed into each other. The word دَكَّ means to smash something against something else until it becomes completely pulverized, turned into powder or made completely smooth. It's the same word used for crushing wheat into flour before water is added to make dough – the process of beating and crushing grain into fine powder.

Imagine the scene: massive portions of earth and whole mountains suspended in the air, then violently smashed together, reduced to dust and powder. And then what happens to all this debris?

translation: Then on that Day, the Resurrection will occur
commentary:
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ

On that day, الْوَاقِعَةُ – that which is bound to fall – falls. First there was a raising, then a crushing, and now the debris is falling. The word الْوَاقِعَةُ means "the incident" or "the inevitable event," but the imagery established in the previous ayah makes the meaning visceral: everything that was raised and destroyed is now falling, crashing down. That which was destined to fall is falling.

But this describes only what's happening to the earth. Now the focus shifts upward.

translation: And the heaven will split [open], for that Day it is infirm
commentary:
وَٱنشَقَّتِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ فَهِىَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ

The sky – that which is above – begins to tear and rip. The verb structure وَٱنشَقَّتِ is particularly significant. This is the form of the verb that indicates something happening of itself, internally. If the passive form of the transitive verb were used, it would suggest an unknown doer, someone making the sky crack. But this form indicates the sky cracks open on its own.

What does this mean? The sky was designed to do this. The responsible party is the sky itself. Built into its design is a timer, and when that timer runs out, it will tear – just as a wound-up toy that runs until its timer expires and then springs open or starts moving. The sky is on a timer, programmed to split when its time comes.

After splitting, فَهِىَ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاهِيَةٌ – on that day it will have weak joints. The word وَاهِيَةٌ means weakness in the joints – weak knees, weak elbows, unable to support weight.

Consider that in Quranic language, the sky is described as a ceiling. Now imagine this ceiling in terms of construction: it's held up by joints and beams. Picture those joints beginning to weaken, the beams starting to shake. You can see the structure trembling, hear the creaking sounds. You know exactly what's coming next – collapse.

The image is like old construction where two planks of wood are tied together, but now they're coming loose. The foundation has already shaken, so now what's above you is shaking. It's cracked in the middle, torn, and about to come crashing down.

translation: And the angels are at its edges. And there will bear the Throne of your Lord above them, that Day, eight [of them]
commentary:
وَٱلْمَلَكُ عَلَىٰٓ أَرْجَآئِهَا ۚ وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَـٰنِيَةٌ

When a building is about to collapse during an earthquake, where do people go? If they can't escape outside, they head to the corners, between door beams, to supporting walls – the strongest parts of the structure. If everything else falls, maybe the beam will hold. Or they head outward, toward the perimeter, hoping to get out before total collapse. Everyone instinctively moves to the outer edges.

The outer perimeter, the outer ends of a structure, is called أَرْجَآءِ. Now we're told that the angels are على أرجائها – at the very ends, on the outer perimeter of the sky. Even the angels are terrified as the sky is about to fall.

However, there is one thing that never falls. There are some angels who can never run. Above all the angels who flee to the edges, the Throne of your Master is held up by eight. These eight angels are assigned to carry the Throne of Allah ﷻ, and they don't budge. While everything else in creation trembles and collapses, the Throne remains, supported by these eight who never waver.

translation: That Day, you will be exhibited [for judgement]; not hidden among you is anything concealed
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ تُعْرَضُونَ لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ

On that day, you will all be brought out and presented. The phrase لَا تَخْفَىٰ مِنكُمْ خَافِيَةٌ emphasizes totality: not a single thing that anyone tried to hide will remain hidden. Nothing concealed will stay concealed. Complete exposure, complete transparency before Allah ﷻ.

translation: So as for he who is given his record in his right hand, he will say, "Here, read my record
commentary:
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ هَآؤُمُ ٱقْرَءُوا۟ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ

Now the scene shifts to individual judgments. The word مَن (whoever) is beautiful here. There are so many great people in human history who are unknown, who will receive their book in their right hand. They're not celebrities, not famous scholars, they don't have worldly power or recognition. Maybe they were janitors somewhere, farmers in remote villages, perhaps they were homeless. The word مَن (whoever) captures this ambiguity – these are nobodies in worldly terms.

But on that Day, when such a person receives their book in the right hand, they can finally show what they accomplished. And he says هَآؤُمُ ٱقْرَءُوا۟ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ – "Here! Everyone, read my book!"

The expression هَآؤُمُ is remarkable. هَاءُ (ha'u) is used to get attention, while the meem is a connecting sound that also suggests plurality – "Hey everybody!" The ه at the end of كِتَـٰبِيَهْ is a stylistic addition suggesting how full of energy and enthusiasm he is. There's joy in it, excitement. Just as expressions of sorrow might add a prolonged sound, here the added sound conveys his exhilaration.

Picture him going around: "Hey, look at this book! Let me show you page five – accepted deeds! Page six – accepted! Page seven – look what you got!" He's like that student who scores perfectly on an exam and sits next to someone who barely passed. The one who failed folds up his exam and sits quietly, while the one who excelled opens it at an angle, going over it as if checking for bonus points, making sure the struggling student can see every mark. "So what did you get? What about this question?"

On Judgment Day, you get to do this. And it's not like you're unaware of others' results – you know very well who passed and who failed. Even in this world, we can often tell by the body language: folded paper stuck in the armpit has a certain look. On Judgment Day, it's even more obvious: people holding their book in their right hand, and people holding their book in their left hand behind their backs, trying to hide it.

But the successful one rubs it in their faces, showing his book openly.

translation: Indeed, I was certain that I would be meeting my account
commentary:
إِنِّى ظَنَنتُ أَنِّى مُلَـٰقٍ حِسَابِيَهْ

This is the "I knew it!" moment. When you get a good report card, there's that rush of validation: "Yes! I knew it!" He says إِنِّى ظَنَنتُ – I was completely convinced, I was so sure – أَنِّى مُلَـٰقٍ حِسَابِيَهْ – that I was absolutely going to meet my accounting, that my accounts would be solid, that I would meet these rewards.

"I knew it! I knew it!" He's cheering for himself.

Remember earlier passages where believers walk with light emanating from their chests and in their right hands, but they're still nervous, still praying "Our Lord, complete our light for us, let us through"? They're worried until the very end. But then this moment comes – the book is handed, everything is confirmed, you're safe now – and suddenly total confidence floods in: "Yes! I knew this would be awesome!"

translation: So he will be in a pleasant life
commentary:
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ

Now begins the description of his reward. فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ – he is living a life that is radiyah.

The word عِيشَةٍ doesn't simply mean "to live" – it means to live large, to live well. The root حَيَا means basic living, but عِيشَةٍ means a good life, an expansive life, living comfortably.

And this life is رَّاضِيَةٍ. This doesn't just mean a life full of pleasure – it means a life that pleases him, a life that is itself a source of contentment. The structure suggests both that he is pleased with this life and that the life itself brings him satisfaction. Everything about his existence now is pleasing.

translation: In an elevated garden
commentary:
فِى جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ

This good life is in جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ – a garden that is high, elevated. What's the significance of a high garden?

Think about why people spend thousands of dollars to visit places like Yosemite National Park, or climb mountains for scenic views, or travel to Mount Rainier near Seattle. They go for the breathtaking view of trees and flowers seen from above. The higher you climb, the more beautiful life appears. You stand there wishing you could just stay in that moment, surrounded by that beauty.

He lives there. The stunning view isn't a photograph in his house or a vacation memory – he simply walks outside and sees this incredible panorama. The garden is very high, elevated, and he dwells there permanently, surrounded by beauty in every direction.

translation: Its [fruit] to be picked hanging near
commentary:
قُطُوفُهَا دَانِيَةٌ

The trees in this garden have قُطُوفُهَا – fruit that is perfectly ripe. The fruit is never unripe or past its prime; it's always at the perfect stage for eating. And it's دَانِيَةٌ – lowering down, hanging within easy reach, ready for picking.

Imagine: you step out to enjoy that breathtaking elevated view, and right there, without effort, perfectly ripe fruit hangs within reach. No climbing, no stretching, no difficulty. This is the epitome of the good life.

translation: [They will be told], "Eat and drink in satisfaction for what you put forth in the days past
commentary:
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَآ أَسْلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَيَّامِ ٱلْخَالِيَةِ

"Eat and drink in satisfaction for what you put forth in the days past."

The believers are told to eat and drink in comfort, satisfaction and peace because of what they أَسْلَفْتُمْ - what they put forth, what they sent ahead. The word aslafa is beautiful because it carries the meaning of doing good deeds that become a legacy. There's a subtle difference in Arabic between khalaf and salaf. Khalaf refers to the people you leave behind after you pass away. If those children or successors turn out well, they are described as khalaf. If they turn out poorly, they are khalaf as well, but with negative connotations. Salaf, on the other hand, refers to the righteous predecessors and the good you sent ahead of yourself.

The phrase فِى ٱلْأَيَّامِ ٱلْخَالِيَةِ - "in the days past" - uses the word khaliyah, which literally means "emptied out." What a remarkable expression for describing the past. These are days that have been emptied out, days that are now just memories, days that are gone. In modern poetry and literature, people speak of time passing and days becoming fading memories. But to Allah ﷻ, those days that are gone for you are eternal. He recorded your deeds in those days, and now those very deeds have become the fruits you are eating in Paradise.

translation: But as for he who is given his record in his left hand, he will say, "Oh, I wish I had not been given my record
commentary:
وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِشِمَالِهِۦ فَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ

"But as for he who is given his record in his left hand, he will say, 'Oh, I wish I had not been given my record.'"

The contrast is stark. While the people of the right hand rejoice, the one who receives his book in his left hand immediately cries out: يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُوتَ كِتَـٰبِيَهْ - "I wish I was never given this book!" Notice that we haven't even discussed punishment yet. The mere possession of this book in his left hand is enough to fill him with dread. He's begging not to be handed this record. "Why do I have this book in my hand? Please don't give it to me!" The regret begins not with the punishment itself, but with the evidence of his own deeds being placed before him.

translation: And had not known what is my account
commentary:
وَلَمْ أَدْرِ مَا حِسَابِيَهْ

"And had not known what is my account."

وَلَمْ أَدْرِ مَا حِسَابِيَهْ - "I wish I had never known what my account was." He wishes he had remained in complete ignorance about what was coming. Because now that he has some clue about what awaits him, the terror has set in. The realization of accountability, of facing the consequences of every deed, becomes unbearable.

translation: I wish my death had been the decisive one
commentary:
يَـٰلَيْتَهَا كَانَتِ ٱلْقَاضِيَةَ

"I wish it had been the decisive one."

يَـٰلَيْتَهَا كَانَتِ ٱلْقَاضِيَةَ - "I wish that [first death] had been the final one." Al-qadiyah refers to something decisive, something that ends the matter completely. He's referring to his death in the worldly life - that first death. "I wish that death had been the end of everything. I wish there was no resurrection. I wish I had died and never came back to life. If only it was over and done with, like when you punch someone and the case is closed - al-qadiyah."

translation: My wealth has not availed me
commentary:
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنِّى مَالِيَهْ ۜ

"My wealth has not availed me."

مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنِّى مَالِيَهْ - "My wealth did nothing for me here." The word مَالِيَهْ with the extra ha at the end adds emphasis - "this wealth of mine." It gave me no benefit whatsoever. I can't pay any fines to get out of this situation. I can't bribe any guards in Hellfire to secure my release. None of my wealth came to my aid. Some scholars also read this as "How did my wealth benefit me?" - a rhetorical question expressing the complete futility of worldly riches in the Hereafter.

translation: Gone from me is my authority
commentary:
هَلَكَ عَنِّى سُلْطَـٰنِيَهْ

"Gone from me is my authority."

هَلَكَ عَنِّى سُلْطَـٰنِيَهْ - "My authority has been destroyed." This person was not just wealthy but also powerful. All the authority, all the status, all the influence has been completely stripped away. Notice the verb Allah ﷻ chose: هَلَكَ - "destroyed" or "killed." The expected verb might have been ذَهَبَ - "went away" or "was removed." But Allah ﷻ uses the stronger word halaka. When something is merely removed, it can potentially be recovered. When something is destroyed or killed, there's no bringing it back. None of that power, none of that authority remains. It has been utterly annihilated.

translation: [Allah will say], "Seize him and shackle him
commentary:
خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ

"[The angels will be commanded], 'Seize him and shackle him.'"

خُذُوهُ فَغُلُّوهُ - "Grab him and chain him up!" The command is given to the angels. Ghulluhu refers to placing iron collars and shackles around the neck. If you've seen images or heard stories about how slaves were kept in chains, those heavy collars around their necks - that's ghull. The word also relates to ghill (prison), as both involve confinement and restriction. He's being seized and bound.

translation: Then into Hellfire drive him
commentary:
ثُمَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ

"Then into Hellfire drive him."

ثُمَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ صَلُّوهُ - "Then, after that, throw him into Jaheem" - into the roaring fire. The word صَلُّوهُ means to expose to intense heat, to roast, to throw into burning flames. After being shackled, he is hurled into the depths of the Fire.

translation: Then into a chain whose length is seventy cubits insert him
commentary:
ثُمَّ فِى سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَٱسْلُكُوهُ

"Then into a chain whose length is seventy cubits insert him."

ثُمَّ فِى سِلْسِلَةٍ ذَرْعُهَا سَبْعُونَ ذِرَاعًا فَٱسْلُكُوهُ - "Then, after he's thrown into the Fire, place a chain on him." He already has a collar on his neck from the previous command. Now a chain is attached to it - a chain whose length is seventy cubits. He's been thrown down from above, so where is this chain coming from? From above. He's suspended, hanging by this chain in the Fire. فَٱسْلُكُوهُ - "Keep him tied, keep him connected, keep him bound to it."

translation: Indeed, he did not used to believe in Allah, the Most Great
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

"Indeed, he did not used to believe in Allah, the Most Great."

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ - "He was the one who did not believe in Allah, the Great." Notice the attribute chosen here: al-'Adheem - the Great, the Magnificent. This person denied the greatness of Allah ﷻ in the worldly life, and now he faces the consequences of that denial.

translation: Nor did he encourage the feeding of the poor
commentary:
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ

"Nor did he encourage the feeding of the poor."

وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ - "And he didn't even encourage feeding the poor." This is profound. Allah ﷻ doesn't say he didn't feed the poor himself - He says he didn't even encourage others to do it. He didn't incline anyone else toward this act of goodness. We've already learned that this person had wealth and power, which comes with immense responsibility. 

When Allah ﷻ gives you more than others, you should be more grateful than others. But this person was so obsessed with his own greatness that he forgot about Allah al-'Adheem. Furthermore, because Allah ﷻ gave him more, he should have been giving more to others, taking care of those who cannot help themselves - the miskeen, the destitute. Not only did he fail to give, he didn't even encourage giving.

This reveals a social phenomenon: when you encourage others to give, the natural question arises - "What have you given?" The finger of accountability points back at you. So he thought, "If I don't talk about charity, people won't ask me about my contributions. Let's have an unspoken agreement - I won't speak about this, you don't ask me, and I won't ask you." Why would he be expected to speak about it? Because he had wealth and power. He was a public figure. He was in a position where people looked to him for leadership on such matters.

translation: So there is not for him here this Day any devoted friend
commentary:
فَلَيْسَ لَهُ ٱلْيَوْمَ هَـٰهُنَا حَمِيمٌ

"So there is not for him here this Day any devoted friend."

فَلَيْسَ لَهُ ٱلْيَوْمَ هَـٰهُنَا حَمِيمٌ - "There's nobody here today for him who is a close friend." Think about it: when rich and powerful people don't help others, do they have genuine friends? Do the masses love the wealthy who never extend a helping hand? When such people lose their wealth, do people sympathize with them? No. People don't feel sorry for millionaires who become thousandaires. There's no love lost. Here in the Hereafter, this reality becomes crystal clear - he has no hameem, no close friend, no intimate companion to support him.

translation: Nor any food except from the discharge of wounds
commentary:
وَلَا طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ

"Nor any food except from the discharge of wounds."

وَلَا طَعَامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ غِسْلِينٍ - "And there's no food for him either, except ghisleen." What is ghisleen? It comes from the root word ghasl, which means "to wash." If you've ever done dishes by hand - and there are many dishes - you put that strainer in the sink to catch debris. When you're done washing all the dishes and you remove that strainer, what's left at the bottom? That disgusting gunk that makes you recoil. That's ghisleen. It's the filthy residue left after washing, whether it's leftover food particles or the dirty water after washing soiled clothes. Imagine a mother cleaning her children's filthy clothes, and the murky, contaminated water collecting in a bucket - that's ghisleen. That leftover discharge from the wash becomes his food.

translation: None will eat it except the sinners
commentary:
لَّا يَأْكُلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْخَـٰطِـُٔونَ

"None will eat it except the sinners."

لَّا يَأْكُلُهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْخَـٰطِـُٔونَ - "Nobody is going to be eating this except the khaati'oon - people who made grave mistakes, people of serious errors." Al-khaati'oon are those who committed major sins, those whose actions were so wrong they warrant this severe punishment.

translation: So I swear by what you see
commentary:
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ

"So I swear by what you see."

فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ - "So I swear by what you can see." Now we need to understand the structure of this oath. The word لَا here isn't a negation of the sentence the way it would be in "I don't go" (لَا أَذْهَبُ) or "I don't sleep" (لَا أَنَامُ). Rather, فَلَا here functions as two separate elements. Think of it as "لَا!" followed by "أُقْسِمُ" - an exclamation, then the oath.

This لَا is used when the beliefs and ideas of the people you're addressing are so fundamentally wrong, so grossly mistaken, that you cannot even begin the conversation without first declaring that all their assumptions are entirely false. Before presenting your argument, you start with a single word - لَا - to say: "All of your philosophies about the afterlife, all of your ideas about what happens when you die, all of your opinions about this man Muhammad ﷺ - all of it is wrong. No. Wrong. Now let me begin."

After this powerful negation, Allah ﷻ says: "I swear by what you can see with your own eyes." Allah ﷻ is making evidence out of what they can physically observe. In this Surah, Allah ﷻ has shown us things they could see and things they could not see. What could they see? The destroyed ruins of ancient nations. They passed by these remnants on their trade routes. But what couldn't they see? Judgment Day. Mountains and earth being crushed together - that was beyond their vision.

There's another dimension to this. In terms of guidance, both the seen and unseen should guide them. The visible ruins of old nations should be lessons. The stories of Nuh (AS) that they've heard should guide them. But even what they cannot see - like the reality of Judgment Day - should also guide them through faith.

Who do they see in front of them calling them to guidance? The Prophet ﷺ. What don't they see? They don't see the angels. They don't see Allah ﷻ. They don't see the source of revelation. They only see this man. Allah ﷻ is saying: "I am calling as witness and proof against you both what you see - meaning this man and his impeccable behavior - and what you don't see."

translation: And what you do not see
commentary:
وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ

"And what you do not see."

وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ - "And what you don't see." What you see is a man. What you don't see is the original source - the revelation itself, the angel Jibreel (AS) delivering it. You don't see these realities, but I'm telling you, I'm calling both of them as witnesses.

This could also mean: what you see and what you hear. You don't see the revelation being delivered. You only hear it. You don't see the preserved scrolls in the heavens. You only hear their recitation. Yet I'm making them proof against you. The visible character of the Prophet ﷺ and the audible words of revelation - together they form an undeniable proof.

translation: [That] indeed, the Qur'an is the word of a noble Messenger
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ

"Indeed, the Qur'an is the word of a noble Messenger."

إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ - "No doubt about it, this is the word of a noble messenger." This is the subject of the oath. Notice the word قَوْل - "word" or "speech" - rather than كَلَام.

There's a subtle but significant difference in Arabic. Kalam refers to one's own speech, speech that originates from the speaker. Qawl refers to what you say, but it may not originate from you. For example, when I recite an ayah, the ayah becomes my qawl - I'm saying it - but it's definitely not my kalam. It's not my own speech; it's the kalam of Allah ﷻ.

This distinction is beautiful. Allah ﷻ says: "This is the qawl of a noble messenger." He doesn't say: "This is the kalam of a noble messenger," because whose kalam is the Quran? It's the kalam of Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ is the noble messenger who delivers it. He's the means by which Allah's kalam becomes his qawl. The nobility (kareem) of this messenger has already been emphasized, showing his trustworthiness and honor as a deliverer of divine speech.

translation: And it is not the word of a poet; little do you believe
commentary:
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ

"And it is not the word of a poet; little do you believe."

وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ - "It's not the word of a poet at all. This is not poetry." Why is the word "poetry" being used here? Listen to this: when you hear these ayahs in recitation, you're taken on a journey. There's a rhythm, a tone, a divine cadence to it. I hesitate to use the word "music" because that has its own connotations, but there's a divine harmony, a sacred flow to the Quran.

Consider the story of Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) before he accepted Islam. He wanted to harm the Prophet ﷺ, and one night he came to the Kabah knowing the Prophet ﷺ prayed there. He hid behind the veil of the Kabah, sneaking around until only the curtain separated him from the Prophet ﷺ. He planned to jump out and attack him. But the Prophet ﷺ was reciting Surah al-Haqqah - this very Surah.

Umar (RA) still hadn't jumped out. He was listening, and he found himself thinking: "Wow, that's some beautiful poetry. The rhythm of it, the flow of it, the speech of it - this is amazing." He was experiencing the Quran. That's what the Quran is meant for - not just intellectual analysis, but experience. You experience Quran in Salah, when you're reciting it, when you're listening to its recitation.

So Umar (RA) was thinking this was magnificent poetry, and the Messenger ﷺ continued reciting until he reached the ayah: وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ - "It's not the word of a poet." Umar (RA) hadn't said anything out loud. He was just thinking it. It was as if the Prophet ﷺ was reading his mind.

قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ - "How little you have faith. How little belief you demonstrate."

translation: Nor the word of a soothsayer; little do you remember
commentary:
وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ

"Nor the word of a soothsayer; little do you remember."

Umar (RA) was shocked. His next thought was: "He's not a poet, but maybe he's a kahin - a fortune teller, a mind reader." Then came the next ayah: وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ - "And not the word of a kahin either."

The kahin were people in pre-Islamic Arabia who claimed supernatural knowledge. People would come to them and ask, "What is my wife thinking about me?" or "Does my husband really love me?" These fortune tellers would put on a show - "Let me call on the spirits..." - and make things up. People would pay money and walk away convinced: "So true! How did you know?" Unfortunately, such practices still exist today, even on television.

قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ - "How little effort you make to remember. Try to think a little harder. Can't you realize what this is?" The word تَذَكَّرُونَ  is critical. It's not about learning something new - it's about remembering, about recognition. Why? Because deep inside each of us is the ruh, the soul, that already knows what this is. When you awaken your ruh, that's not called "awareness" - it's called "remembrance." You need to be jolted and reminded, and then it finally clicks with your ruh. Your heart wakes up and says, "Oh, that's what this is!"

Umar (RA) heard this and couldn't take it anymore. He came with the intention to beat up the Prophet ﷺ, but he ran away, his heart cracking open to the truth.

translation: [It is] a revelation from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"[It is] a revelation from the Lord of the worlds."

تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "A revelation from the Master of all nations, the Lord of all worlds." This is not conjecture, not poetry, not fortune-telling. This is tanzeel - a systematic, deliberate revelation descending from the Rabb of all creation.

## Ayahs 44-47

وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ
لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِٱلْيَمِينِ
ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ ٱلْوَتِينَ
فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَـٰجِزِينَ

"And if he had made up about Us some [false] sayings, We would have seized him by the right hand; then We would have cut from him the aorta. And there is no one of you who could prevent [Us] from him."

Allah ﷻ now addresses how seriously He takes the integrity of His revelation. The word تَقَوَّلَ means to fabricate speech, to craft and adapt words deliberately, to put effort into creating impressive speech. It implies rehearsal, practice, creative exercise.

وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ - "Had he" - meaning the Prophet ﷺ, that noble messenger whose nobility has already been established - "had he rehearsed some words, had he practiced some statements to impress you, had he decided in the heat of the moment to add just a word or two of his own creation to this speech..."

Notice: not the entire speech, just بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ - "some of the words, just some of them." Even less than nine words. Even just a small addition - a verb here, a phrase there, nothing major.

لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِٱلْيَمِينِ - "We would grab him with Our right hand." The word يَمِين means both "right hand" and "power." Allah ﷻ would seize him with overwhelming power. When you grab an animal by the right hand to slaughter it, that's the imagery here - a firm, decisive grip.

ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ ٱلْوَتِينَ - "Then We would cut from him the watīn" - the jugular vein, the aorta, right here at the base of the throat. This is where words come from. If these words were sourced from him and not from revelation, Allah ﷻ would cut off the source - his throat, his neck.

Think about it: the Messenger ﷺ himself had to recite these ayahs. He had to convey Allah's words about what would happen to him if he fabricated even a portion of revelation.

فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَـٰجِزِينَ - "And not one of you would be able to come on his behalf to serve as a barrier." حَاجِز means a barrier, someone who prevents or protects. Allah ﷻ is saying: "Nobody - not a single person - would be able to stop Me from carrying out this punishment on him."

This is Allah's guarantee of the Quran's preservation and authenticity. If the Prophet ﷺ had altered even the smallest part of the revelation, immediate and severe consequences would have followed, and no one could have prevented it.

translation: And if Muhammad had made up about Us some [false] sayings
We would have seized him by the right hand
Then We would have cut from him the aorta
And there is no one of you who could prevent [Us] from him
And indeed, the Qur'an is a reminder for the righteous
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

"And indeed, it is a reminder for the righteous."

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - "And no doubt about it, this is a powerful and truly effective reminder for people who want to protect themselves, for people who want to have taqwa." This Quran serves as a tadhkirah - a reminder that awakens the soul of those who seek to be conscious of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And indeed, We know that among you are deniers
commentary:
وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنكُم مُّكَذِّبِينَ

"And indeed, We know that among you are deniers."

وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنكُم مُّكَذِّبِينَ - "And We absolutely know for sure that among you are those who are going to call this a lie, no matter what." Despite all the evidence, despite the miraculous nature of the Quran, despite the character of the Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ acknowledges that some will persist in denial. وَمِنكُم - "And they are among you."

translation: And indeed, it will be [a cause of] regret upon the disbelievers
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

"And indeed, it is a source of regret upon the disbelievers."

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "And no doubt about it, this is going to become a source of great grief and regret for the disbelievers." When will this hasrah - this intense regret - occur? In two places: when worldly punishment comes, and when the punishment of the Hereafter arrives.

Think about when Makkah was conquered and Surah an-Nasr was revealed - that was hasrah for those who opposed the truth. But the ultimate hasrah will be in the afterlife, when the full reality becomes undeniable.

translation: And indeed, it is the truth of certainty
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ

"And indeed, it is the truth of certainty."

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ - "And no doubt about it, this is haqq al-yaqeen" - the absolute truth, the conviction based on certainty, the reality confirmed through experience. This phrase brings us back to the beginning of the Surah - al-Haqqah, the Inevitable Reality. Haqq al-yaqeen signifies certainty that comes through direct experience and witnessing, the highest level of conviction.

translation: So exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great
commentary:
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

"So exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great."

فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ - "Then declare the perfection of your great Master by using His name. Use the name of your Master to declare His perfection, to declare how great He is."

The word رَبِّكَ could describe اسْم - "Your Lord's name" - or it could describe الْعَظِيمِ - "Your Lord who is Great." In this eloquent construction, it describes both: the great name of your great Master.

You call on that name to declare His perfection - that's tasbih. But why does the Surah end with this command about tasbih when the preceding conversation was about believing in the messenger and believing in the Quran? These seem like separate subjects.

The connection is profound: any accusation against this messenger is actually an attack against the perfection of Allah ﷻ. The Quran and the sending of this messenger are themselves proof of Allah's perfection. This is the perfect revelation, the perfect guidance, the perfect deen. Therefore, any attack against the Prophet ﷺ is a violation of Allah's tasbih, a violation of Allah's rights, an assault on Allah's perfection.

So when you declare Allah's greatness - when you engage in tasbih using His name - you do so by believing in this revelation and by accepting this messenger for who he truly is. Earlier, when describing the person thrown into Hellfire, Allah ﷻ said: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لَا يُؤمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ - "He didn't believe in Allah, the Great." Now Allah ﷻ tells us: if you don't want to be in that place, then declare the greatness of Allah by His name, by honoring His revelation, and by following His Messenger ﷺ.
